en.extract:
The white-collared starling (Grafisia torquata) is a species of starling in the family Sturnidae. It is monotypic within the genus Grafisia.  It is found in Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad, Democratic Republic of the Congo, and Gabon.


Taxonomy
The species was first identified by Anton Reichenow and named Spreo torquatus, from the Latin for "torquated", referring to the coloration around the neck of the male. It was later identified independently by James Chapin in 1913 in the Belgian Congo as Stilbopsar leucothorax, from the Greek λευκός (meaning "white") and θώραξ (meaning "chest"), again referring to its white collar. They were placed into its current genus Grafisia by George Latimer Bates in 1926, based on substantial differences between the white-collared starling and members of either Spreo or Stilbopsar.


Description
White-collared starlings are dimorphic in adulthood, with sexual differences in both size and coloration. Adult males have a glossy-black plumage save for a white patch on the chest which extends up to the wings; their bill and legs are black and a bright yellow iris. Adult females are primarily grey with black-tipped wing and tail feathers; the feathers on its crown and rump have blue-black tips. Juveniles and subadults tend to resemble the female, but with more brownish-grey feathers on their ventral plumage.
The white-collared starling has been reported to have both a chirruping and a call of three short whistled notes.


Behavior
White-collared starlings are generally found in open woodland and seem to stay in the tops of high trees, but have also been reported in montane grasslands in Cameroon They are known to feed on insects, berries, wild figs and the fruit of musanga and macaranga assass trees.
They have been known to travel in pairs or small groups (4-10), and in at least one case in a flock of more than 100 birds in June. Nest-building activity has been observed in March, and males collected at that time were found to have large testes; these facts combined with the appearance of juvenile species between May and July suggest that breeding occurs in the first half of the year. In one instance, a male was seen to make short circular flights from a conspicuous pearch, which was interpreted as a courtship display.


References


External links
White-collared Starling at the Macaulay Library - Pictures, videos and location information
White-collared Starling factsheet at Birdlife International

The '''white-collared starling''' ('''''Grafisia torquata''''') is a species of [[starling]] in the family [[Sturnidae]]. It is [[monotypic]] within the genus '''''Grafisia'''''.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.itis.gov/servlet/SingleRpt/SingleRpt?search_topic=TSN&search_value=557705 |title=ITIS Report: ''Grafisia'' |access-date=28 June 2012 |publisher=[[Integrated Taxonomic Information System]]}}</ref>  It is found in [[Cameroon]], [[Central African Republic]], [[Chad]], [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], and [[Gabon]].<ref name="iucn status 11 November 2021" />

==Taxonomy==
The species was first identified by [[Anton Reichenow]] and named ''Spreo torquatus'', from the Latin for "torquated", referring to the coloration around the neck of the male.<ref name="Reichenow1909">{{cite journal |last=Reichenow |first=Anton |author-link=Anton Reichenow |title=Neue Arten von Kamerun |journal=Ornithologische Monatsberichte |volume=17 |year=1909 |page=140 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32582643 |access-date=2013-10-25}}</ref> It was later identified independently by [[James Chapin]] in 1913 in the Belgian Congo as ''Stilbopsar leucothorax'',<ref name="Chapin1954">{{cite journal |last=Chapin |first=James P. |year=1954 |journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History |title=The birds of the Belgian Congo Part 4 |volume=75b |pages=137–138 |hdl=2246/1643 }}</ref> from the Greek λευκός (meaning "white") and θώραξ (meaning "chest"), again referring to its white collar. They were placed into its current genus ''Grafisia'' by [[George Latimer Bates]] in 1926, based on substantial differences between the white-collared starling and members of either ''[[Spreo]]'' or ''[[Poeoptera|Stilbopsar]]''.<ref name="Bates1926">{{cite journal |journal=[[Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club]] |year=1926 |volume=46 |page=104 |last=Bates |first=George Latimer |author-link=George Latimer Bates |url=https://archive.org/details/bulletinofbritis46tayl |title=Mr G.L. Bates described a new genus of starling from N.W. Cameroon |access-date=2013-10-25}}</ref>

==Description==
White-collared starlings are [[Sexual dimorphism|dimorphic]] in adulthood, with sexual differences in both size and coloration. Adult males have a glossy-black plumage save for a white patch on the chest which extends up to the wings; their bill and legs are black and a bright yellow iris. Adult females are primarily grey with black-tipped wing and tail feathers; the feathers on its crown and rump have blue-black tips. Juveniles and subadults tend to resemble the female, but with more brownish-grey feathers on their ventral plumage.<ref name="Feare1999">{{cite book |last1=Feare |first1=Chris |last2=Craig |first2=Adrian |date=1999-01-18 |title=Starlings and Mynas |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton, NJ |isbn=978-0-691-00496-9 |pages=249–250}}</ref>

The white-collared starling has been reported to have both a ''chirruping'' and a call of three short whistled notes.<ref name="Bannerman1948">{{cite book |first=David A |last=Bannerman |author-link=David Armitage Bannerman |year=1948 |title=The Birds of Tropical West Africa |publisher=Oliver and Boyd |location=Edinburg and London}}</ref>

==Behavior==
White-collared starlings are generally found in open woodland and seem to stay in the tops of high trees, but have also been reported in [[Montane grasslands and shrublands|montane grasslands]] in Cameroon<ref name="Louette1981">{{cite book |last=Louette |first=Michel |year=1981 |title=The Birds of Cameroon: An Annotated Checklist |series=Verhandelingen van de Koninklijke Academie voor Wetenschappen, Letteren en Schone Kunsten van Belgie, Klasse der Wetenschappen |issue=163 |volume=43 |isbn=978-90-6569-656-4}}</ref> They are known to feed on [[insects]], [[berries]],<ref name="Bannerman1948"/> wild [[figs]] and the fruit of ''[[Musanga cecropioides|musanga]]''<ref name="Chapin1954"/> and ''[[Macaranga|macaranga assass]]'' trees.<ref name="DowsettLemaire1996">{{cite journal | last=Dowsett-Lemaire |first=F |title=Avian frugivore assemblages at three small-fruited tree species in the forests of northern Congo. |journal=[[Ostrich (journal)|Ostrich]] |volume=67 |issue=2 |pages=88–89 |year=1996 |doi=10.1080/00306525.1996.9633788}}</ref>

They have been known to travel in pairs or small groups (4-10),<ref name="Germain1973">{{cite journal |last1=Germain |first1=Max  |last2=Dragesco |first2=J. |last3=Roux |first3=F |last4=Garcin |first4=H. |trans-title=Contribution to the Ornithology of South Cameroon: 2. Passeriformes |title=Contribution à l'ornithologie du Sud-Cameroun: 2. Passeriformes |journal=L'Oiseau et la Revue Française d'Ornithologie |year=1973 |volume=43 |issue=3 |pages=212–259 |url=http://www.documentation.ird.fr/hor/fdi:18371 |language=French |access-date=2013-10-25}}</ref> and in at least one case in a flock of more than 100 birds in June. Nest-building activity has been observed in March, and males collected at that time were found to have large testes;<ref name="Serle1965">{{cite journal |title=A Third Contribution to the Ornithology of the British Cameroons |journal=Ibis |volume=107 |issue=2 |pages=230–246 |last=Serle |first=William |year=1965 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1965.tb07298.x}}</ref> these facts combined with the appearance of juvenile species between May and July suggest that breeding occurs in the first half of the year. In one instance, a male was seen to make short circular flights from a conspicuous pearch, which was interpreted as a courtship display.<ref name="Feare1999"/><ref name="Bannerman1948"/>
